channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Honda Scraps Plans for E.V.s While Start-Ups Forge Ahead
item.description: The Japanese company’s retreat echoes moves by other traditional carmakers as the industry divides between electric vehicle haves and have-nots.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Honda once planned to retool its plant in Marysville, Ohio, to make electric vehicles. The company announced this week that it was scrapping plans for three battery-powered models.

item.title: How the Iran War Threatens the Global Food, Energy and Other Supplies
item.description: Beyond its effects on oil and gas, the unfolding war in the Middle East is roiling shipping and airfreight, threatening the availability of a vast range of goods.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The container terminal in Newark.

item.title: What to Know About the U.S. Lasers Being Used to Counter Iranian Attacks
item.description: High-energy lasers can burn drones in the sky like a blowtorch. But they can be stymied by weather, and making them at scale will be a challenge.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Nic Antaya for The New York Times
item.media:description: A high-energy laser on display during a manufacturing conference in Detroit last year.

item.title: Surging Energy Costs Put German Industry ‘Really in Danger’
item.description: Four years after the Russian invasion of Ukraine sent energy prices soaring, the war in Iran is posing another challenge to efforts to revive European factories.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson and Andreas Meichsner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs

item.title: Oil Prices Barely Budge After Trump Lifts Russia Sanctions
item.description: After surging about 10 percent on Thursday, oil prices had little reaction to the decision by President Trump to waive sanctions on the sale of some Russian crude.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: War in Iran Has India Scrambling to Keep Stoves Lit for Cooking
item.description: India relies on huge quantities of cooking gas that is normally shipped from the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B. and Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Workers cooked food using firewood instead of gas outside a restaurant in Bengaluru, India, amid disruptions in the liquid petroleum gas supply.

item.title: With Disputed Legal Maneuver, Trump Tries to Set Policy Without Legislation
item.description: By suing Republican states and making sharp reversals in old cases, the Trump administration is using courts to fast-track major shifts in policy.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In some cases, the legal maneuver has helped the Trump administration lock in changes at the federal level that could persist for years after President Trump leaves office.

item.title: Trump Targets Forced Labor in Global Tariff Scheme
item.description: The Trump administration began a trade investigation Thursday into whether dozens of countries have policies to combat forced labor.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trucks leaving the Port of Los Angeles last May. The latest trade investigation is part of an effort by the Trump administration to resurrect a global system of tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court last month.

item.title: Banking Regulators Plan to Ease Some Capital Requirements
item.description: The Federal Reserve and other financial regulators will soon release proposals to loosen some planned rules on how much capital banks must hold in reserve to mitigate risks.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.media:description: Michelle W. Bowman, the Federal Reserve’s vice chair for supervision, last year.

item.title: The Jewelry Industry Is Taking a Wild Ride as Gold and Silver Prices Soar
item.description: Some jewelers are turning to wood, steel and even platinum. Others are leaning into the volatility with bigger and bolder designs.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Gomelsky

item.title: Noma Could Have Changed So Much More Than Food
item.description: The globally acclaimed restaurant had the power and influence to remake restaurant culture. Instead, it perpetuated the darkest parts of that world.
item.dc:creator: Tejal Rao
item.media:credit: Ditte Isager for The New York Times
item.media:description: Noma opened in 2003 in Copenhagen. Twenty years later, the restaurant announced that it would close to focus on other efforts, like the pop-up it started this week in Los Angeles,

item.title: Fallout From Iran War and Oil Shock Deliver Another Blow to World Economy
item.description: Countries already walloped by a breakdown of the international trading order, war in Ukraine and chaotic U.S. policymaking are facing potentially lasting economic damage.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo ships on Wednesday in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Senate Resoundingly Passes Housing Bill, but Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: The lopsided vote to approve the measure was a rare bit of election-year bipartisanship on a major affordability issue, but G.O.P. disputes and President Trump’s disinterest have left its fate uncertain.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Home construction in South Carolina earlier this year.

item.title: U.S. Mortgage Rates Rise, Undercutting Housing Affordability Push
item.description: Mortgage rates fell below the critical 6 percent threshold just a couple weeks ago. But they’re climbing again as new inflation concerns have roiled financial markets.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Home buyers have already been struggling with high prices, as well as rising insurance and property taxes.

item.title: War With Iran Puts Further Strain on America’s Pessimistic Farmers
item.description: Nearly 75 percent of farmers surveyed before the war said the crop sector was in a recession. They face bigger hurdles this planting season.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: Corn harvesting in Earlham, Iowa, last year. The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran is hurting U.S. farmers because the Middle East is crucial to worldwide fertilizer production.

item.title: Why Does Oil Matter So Much to the Global Economy?
item.description: The war in the Middle East has sent oil and gas prices soaring. Our energy reporter Rebecca F. Elliott breaks down how much the world continues to depend on reliable sources of oil.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Sutton Raphael, Joey Sendaydiego, James Surdam and Nikolay Nikolov

item.title: Iran War Causing Largest Ever Oil Disruption, I.E.A. Says
item.description: Conflict is forcing producers to slash production and close ports as Iran steps up attacks on energy infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: High prices at a gas station in Los Angeles on March 9, 2026, as costs have surged because of the conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Falls in January
item.description: The data showed imports dipped and exports rose in the month before the Supreme Court struck down most of the president’s tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A container terminal at the Port of Los Angeles.

item.title: Private Equity’s Private Credit Problem
item.description: Investors have worried about who would get hit if there’s a private credit bust. Many on Wall Street think the pain goes further than the lenders.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Many on Wall Street worry that lenders like Blue Owl Capital aren’t the only potential casualties of a private credit bust.

item.title: What to Know About Airport Security Lines During the Partial Government Shutdown
item.description: Images of seemingly endless waits at security checkpoints have spread online, but the reality of the partial government shutdown is less straightforward.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: The ongoing partial government shutdown could begin to have a bigger effect at Transportation Security Administration checkpoints after officers’ paychecks stop arriving on Friday.

item.title: Trump Administration Suggests Tariff Refunds May Take Significant Time
item.description: The government must update a federal court on Thursday about its timeline for returning roughly $166 billion in illegal duties.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: To refund any tariffs collected during the most punishing phase of President Trump’s trade war, the government said it would need more time.

item.title: Universal Will Give Movies Longer Exclusive Runs in Theaters
item.description: Theaters will get a minimum of five weekends of exclusive play and then, starting next year, seven. A pandemic-era policy was guaranteeing only three.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Coding After Coders: The End of Computer Programming as We Know It
item.description: In the era of A.I. agents, many Silicon Valley programmers are now barely programming. Instead, what they’re doing is deeply, deeply weird.
item.dc:creator: Clive Thompson

item.title: A Call for Reporting Tips Rankles Pentagon Officials
item.description: A Washington Post appeal for information about the military qualified as prohibited “solicitation,” according to defense officials.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon’s new rules allow it to declare journalists “security risks.”

item.title: Why Oil Prices Surged Even After the Release of Strategic Reserves
item.description: Reserves or no reserves, the outlook remains bleak as long as a major oil and gas trade route remains virtually closed.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil tanker sits anchored as the traffic is down in the Strait of Hormuz in Muscat, Oman, on Tuesday.

item.title: Top-End Auction Sales Help Pull Global Art Market Out of Slump, Study Says
item.description: The top drivers included a $2.2 billion auction week in New York and strong fall fairs, according to the annual Art Basel and UBS report.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Art Basel Paris fair at the Grand Palais last October.

item.title: A Third of Americans Have Cut Spending or Borrowed Money for Health Care
item.description: As medical costs rise, more than 80 million people have made sacrifices like skipping meals and driving less, a new survey finds.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.media:description: A recent poll found that health care costs topped a list of the public’s economic anxieties, above concerns about the prices of food and groceries, gas and utilities.

item.title: How to Afford Housing in London: Multiple Roommates, No Living Room
item.description: High prices have pushed more Londoners to live with roommates well into their late 20s and 30s — if they can afford to move out of their parents’ homes at all.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Darragh pays 900 pounds, or about $1,225, a month for his share of a six-bedroom house in a northwest London neighborhood.

item.title: S&P 500 Slumps in Worst Day Since War Began
item.description: The stock index fell 1.5 percent on Thursday, as concerns continued to grow about oil prices rising amid the war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices over $5 a gallon in Los Angeles, California, on Wednesday. Gas prices rose on Wednesday to a national average of $3.58 a gallon, according to the AAA motor club.

item.title: Pentagon Bars Photographers From Briefings on Iran War
item.description: The decision is the latest move by the Pentagon to restrict access inside the department for journalists.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth walking into the East Room of the White House last week. Mr. Hegseth has taken an adversarial stance toward the news media since his first days of running the Pentagon.

item.title: Noma Faces Los Angeles Protest Over Allegations of Past Abuse by Its Founder
item.description: At the vaunted restaurant’s pop-up in the Silver Lake neighborhood, fallout from recent reports continued.
item.dc:creator: Meghan McCarron and Julia Moskin
item.category: Cadillac Division of General Motors Corp
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the protest he helped organize, Jason Ignacio White, center, read from an open letter to Noma.

item.title: Countries Scramble to Shore Up Global Oil Supplies as Ships Are Attacked
item.description: The United States and Israel launched more strikes against Iran, where crowds mourned military commanders killed in the war. Israel also bombed targets in Lebanon, where the death toll climbed.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Abdi Latif Dahir, Eric Schmitt and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A navy ship from the United Arab Emirates sailed next to a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Targets Major Trading Partners With New Investigation
item.description: A trade investigation focuses on the European Union, China, India and other countries and will likely lead to tariffs to replace those struck down by the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tyler Pager
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:description: The trade investigations would be carried out under a law that allows the United States to impose tariffs on foreign countries in response to certain foreign practices.

item.title: What’s Better Than One Top Oscar Nominee? How About Two?
item.description: Warner Bros. is behind both “One Battle After Another” and “Sinners.” The studio has worked hard to not favor one over the other.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.category: Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences

item.title: Trump’s Actions in Iran and Venezuela Show Limits of U.S. Sanctions
item.description: America’s vast economic powers are able to wear down an adversary’s economy but are insufficient to topple leaders on their own.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Watching the aftermath of a strike in Tehran last week. Having reached the limits of its sanctions tools this year, the Trump administration is opting instead to use military force in places like Iran and Venezuela.

item.title: Consumer prices stayed subdued in February.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: At Least 3 Ships Are Struck Around Strait of Hormuz, and Iran Claims One
item.description: Three vessels were hit in and around the Persian Gulf on Wednesday, according to a British maritime agency. Iran claimed responsibility for one.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Royal Thai Navy, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: An image released by the Royal Thai Navy shows a vessel near the Strait of Hormuz that was attacked on Wednesday. Iran claimed responsibility.

item.title: World Leaders Will Release 400 Million Barrels of Oil to Stabilize Prices
item.description: The members of the International Energy Agency will release 400 million barrels of oil, the largest such coordinated action on record.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Microsoft Takes a Stand Against the Trump Administration in Anthropic Fight
item.description: The technology giant’s siding with Anthropic in its fight against the Pentagon stands out in an era when big companies have tended to keep quiet.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Satya Nadella, the C.E.O. of Microsoft, has been less visible in Washington during President Trump’s second term than some of his fellow tech chiefs.

item.title: A Trump-Xi Summit Nears, but China Doesn’t Know What Trump Wants
item.description: Trump’s agenda for the high-stakes meeting remains unclear to Beijing, Chinese analysts say, while American executives say they haven’t been invited along.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and David Pierson
item.media:description: President Trump and Xi Jinping, China’s leader, meeting in Busan, South Korea, in October, where they announced a yearlong trade truce.

item.title: U.S. Inflation Stayed Subdued Before Onset of Iran War
item.description: While February’s Consumer Price Index report shows only modest price pressures, inflationary risks are rising again as the conflict in the Middle East drags on.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Since February, rising gas prices have added to inflation.
item.description: Gas prices don’t move in lock step with crude, usually trailing increases or drops by a few days.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner

item.title: Trump Directs Iran War Keeping Markets Top of Mind
item.description: President Trump again demonstrated his desire to keep the stock markets aloft when he suggested U.S. attacks on Iran could end soon.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: In Talking to Parents About Vaccines, Pediatricians Navigate a Sea of Misinformation
item.description: Practitioners nationwide are striving to do what’s best for children’s health, while staying supportive in the face of mistrust and confusion.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Maddie McGarvey
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Gas Prices Jump for 11th Straight Day, and Oil Pushes Higher
item.description: Drivers are paying an average of 20 percent more at the pump since the strikes on Iran began. The global oil price was higher and stocks were mixed.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: How Trump and His Advisers Miscalculated Iran’s Response to War
item.description: In the lead-up to the U.S.-Israeli attack, President Trump downplayed the risks to the energy markets as a short-term concern that should not overshadow the mission to decapitate the Iranian regime.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Tyler Pager and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: In response to Iranian threats, commercial shipping has come to a standstill in the Gulf, and oil prices have spiked.

item.title: Has the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement Affected You? We Want to Hear About It.
item.description: The New York Times is looking to talk to people about the impact that the trade deal between the United States, Canada and Mexico has had on them and their businesses.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
